coredata.dc:title: Graphene oxide-assisted synthesis of N, S Co-doped carbon quantum dots for fluorescence detection of multiple heavy metal ions
coredata.dc:creator: Shen, Yujie
Rong, Mingcong
Qu, Xiaodan
Zhao, Bolin
Zou, Jinhui
Liu, Zhenbang
Bao, Yu
He, Ying
Li, Siyi
Wang, Xiaojin
Chen, Meiyun
Chen, Kaiying
Zhang, Yuwei
Niu, Li
coredata.dc:description: Detection of heavy metal ions (HMIs) in water is an important topic in the field of analytical chemistry and environmental science. Fluorescence spectroscopy is one of the most promising strategies due to its simple instrument, low investment, rapid and convenient operation. However, current fluorescence probes for detecting HMIs are typically selective for certain ions. Herein we reported the development of a novel strategy that determined the total content of HMIs in water by fluorescence spectroscopy. A novel fluorescent nitrogen, sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, S-CQDs) was prepared via graphene oxide-assisted synthesis method. The results showed that, with the fluorescence quenching strategy, N, S-CQDs exhibited a wide linear response to a series of water-soluble metal ions. The fluorescence of N, S-CQDs is stable in a wide range of pH 4–11. The detection mechanism was proved that the integration, caused by coordination interaction between S element in N, S-CQDs and the d-orbital of associated metal ions, was the main reason for fluorescence quenching. In practice, the N, S-CQDs were applied to determine total content of HMIs in water successfully. Interestingly, further experiment proved that the N, S-CQDs could effectively remove HMIs in water after centrifuging and filtering thoroughly. It was shown that the fluorescence of N, S-CQDs was obviously quenched by the multiple-ions-involved water and scavenging effect of N, S-CQDs on HMIs with centrifugal in which the concentration of individuals meets the Chinese National Standard. This indicates that the N, S-CQDs are of a wide application prospect in water quality analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multiple heavy metal ions
Fluorescence probe
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Experiment of fluorescent detections of different ions
Determination of removal efficiency of different ions by ICP-MS
Sensitivity of N, S-CQDs for detection of HMIs
Mechanism for detecting metal ions by N, S-CQDs
Application of the N, S-CQDs for detecting ions in real water sample
Application of N, S-CQDs in removing heavy metal ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N, S-CQDs. The inset shows the lattice spacing of N, S-CQDs; (b) The size distribution of N, S-CQDs in (a); (c) TEM image of aggregates formed in detecting Co2+ based on N, S-CQDs; (d) TEM image of aggregates formed in detecting Pb2+ based on N, S-CQDs; (e) Absorption (black line) spectrum, fluorescence excitation spectrum (red line, λem: 400 nm) and fluorescence emission spectrum (green line, λex: 330 nm) of N, S-CQDs. (f) Fluorescence peak intensities of N, S-CQDs solution (0.03 mg/mL) at different pH. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) The fluorescence intensities of N, S-CQDs with different cations (1 mM); (b) The fluorescence intensities of N, S-CQDs with different anions (1 mM); (c–g) Fluorescence quenching-concentration dependence of N, S-CQDs. F0 and ΔF stands for the blank fluorescence intensity and the absolute value of change in fluorescence intensity, respectively. The concentration of N, S-CQDs is 0.03 mg/mL.
FT-IR spectra of graphene oxide, l-cysteine, N, S-CQDs and Pb–N, S-CQDs.

XPS results of (a) C1s, (b) O1s, (c) N1s and (d) S2p of the as-prepared N, S-CQDs, (e) C1s, (f) O1s, (g) N1s and (h) S2p of the mixture of Pb2+ and N, S-CQDs. XPS spectra for other HMIs are included in Supporting Information.

STEM images of Pb–N, S-CQDs (a). Elemental mapping with EDS showing same distribution of Pb (b), O (c), S (e) and N (f) throughout the aggregated Pb–N, S-CQDs, respectively. d is the overlay of a, b, c, e, f.

(a) The schematic illustration of synthesis of N, S-CQDs and fluorescence sensing of HMIs. (b) Photographs of the fluorescence change of N, S-CQDs (0.03 mg/mL) with and without Pb2+ (100 μM) under 365 nm UV lamp.
Performance comparison of different materials for the detection metal ions.

head.ce:title: Graphene oxide-assisted synthesis of N, S Co-doped carbon quantum dots for fluorescence detection of multiple heavy metal ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, 230026, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230026, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Detection of heavy metal ions (HMIs) in water is an important topic in the field of analytical chemistry and environmental science. Fluorescence spectroscopy is one of the most promising strategies due to its simple instrument, low investment, rapid and convenient operation. However, current fluorescence probes for detecting HMIs are typically selective for certain ions. Herein we reported the development of a novel strategy that determined the total content of HMIs in water by fluorescence spectroscopy. A novel fluorescent nitrogen, sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, S-CQDs) was prepared via graphene oxide-assisted synthesis method. The results showed that, with the fluorescence quenching strategy, N, S-CQDs exhibited a wide linear response to a series of water-soluble metal ions. The fluorescence of N, S-CQDs is stable in a wide range of pH 4–11. The detection mechanism was proved that the integration, caused by coordination interaction between S element in N, S-CQDs and the d-orbital of associated metal ions, was the main reason for fluorescence quenching. In practice, the N, S-CQDs were applied to determine total content of HMIs in water successfully. Interestingly, further experiment proved that the N, S-CQDs could effectively remove HMIs in water after centrifuging and filtering thoroughly. It was shown that the fluorescence of N, S-CQDs was obviously quenched by the multiple-ions-involved water and scavenging effect of N, S-CQDs on HMIs with centrifugal in which the concentration of individuals meets the Chinese National Standard. This indicates that the N, S-CQDs are of a wide application prospect in water quality analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution is one of the main environmental issues in modern society [1]. Due to the discharge of industrial wastewater from electroplating, chemical industry and metallurgy, different kinds of heavy metal ions (HMIs) are released into the environment. The HMIs pollution would result in direct damage to aquatic organisms and the food chain [2]. The occurrence of diseases caused by polluted water also increases significantly in recent years [3]. Therefore, it is of great significance to access the content of HMIs in wastewater. Wastewater is always made up of several kinds of HMIs, including Co2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, and Pb2+ etc. Just like the determination of the total content of organic pollutants in water, sometimes we do not need to determine the exact content of a specific ion, but to determine the overall content of HMIs to assess water quality [4]. Sometimes, the toxic effect of the mixture cannot be predicted from the effect of individual components. It usually requires to evaluate the water quality by the overall content of HMIs. However, the concentration of HMIs could only be measured one by one with commonly used methods, e.g. electrochemical approach [5,6], atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [7], atomic fluorescence spectroscopy (AFS) [8] and inductively coupled plasma-mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS) etc. [9] In addition, these methods also have shortcomings such as time-consuming and expensive [10]. By contrast, fluorescence spectroscopy for water quality evaluation is appealing in view of its merits in multi aspects, e.g. simple, low-cost, fast detection, and wide detection range [11]. However, the developed fluorescence probes, such as metal clusters, metal nanoparticles, inorganic quantum dots, usually respond selectively to certain ions [12–17]. Among them, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), zero-dimensional carbon-based materials with the size of 1–10 nm, are potential candidates for multi-ions response fluorescence probe [18]. The detecting properties of CQDs to HMIs can be freely tailored by different functional groups and size-controlling [19–21]. Therefore, in this work, by using l-cysteine as the precursor, taking graphene oxide as an auxiliary reagent, a nitrogen, sulfur co-doped CQDs (N, S-CQDs) were synthesized and used to detect a variety of heavy metal cationic ions, e.g. Co2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ etc. The evaluation of total content of HMIs in wastewater is realized by measuring the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CQDs. In addition, the N, S-CQDs can not only be used to detect HMIs, but also remove HMIs. The mixture of HMIs and N, S-CQDs will precipitate from solution and be removed by centrifugation. In view of the as-achieved excellent detection performance and simple preparation process, the home-made N, S-CQDs displays good application potentials in detection and capture of HMIs in water.

NH3·H2O (AR, 25%–28%) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Company. l-cysteine (97% purity) was purchased from SIGMA-ALDRICH Co., NaH2PO2 (AR, 99.0% purity), KI (AR, 99%), KBr (AR, 99%), Na2MoO4 (AR, 99.0%), Na2SO4 (AR, 98.0%), NH4Cl, NaNO2 (99.99%), CH3COOH (AR, 99.5%), H3BO3 (AR), NaCl (AR, 99.5%), NaF (99.0%), Sodium citrate (NaCA) (≥99.0%), citric acid (≥99.5%) and quinine sulfate (98%) were purchased from Beijing Innochem Science & Technology company. NaNO3 (AR, ≥ 99.0%), Ca(NO3)2 (AR, ≥ 99.0%), Ni(NO3)2 (AR, ≥ 98.0%), Pb(NO3)2 (AR, ≥ 99.0%), Zn(NO3)2 (AR, ≥ 99.0%), Al(NO3)3 (99.0%), KNO3 (AR, ≥ 99.0%), Mg(NO3)2 (AR, ≥ 99.0%) were purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory. Co(NO3)2 (AR, ≥99%) was purchased from ACROS ORGANICS Co., AgNO3(AR, 99.8%) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., NaC2O4(99%) was purchased from Alfa Aesar Co., HgCl2(AR, 99.5%) was purchased at Jiangyan Huanqiu reagent Factory. NaOH (AR, 96.0%), NaH2PO4 (AR, ≥ 99.0%), K2HPO4 and H3PO4 (AR) were purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory; Na2CO3 (AR ≥ 99.5%), Sodium acetate (NaAC) (AR, 99.0%), Cu(NO3)2 (AR), Fe(NO3)3 (AR, ≥ 98.5%) and Cd(NO3)2 (AR, 99.0%) were also purchased from Shanghai Macklin Company. Graphene oxide was prepared by chemical intercalation of pristine graphite according to our previous work [22]. All chemicals were used as received without further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared using deionized water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ/cm.

N, S-CQDs were synthesized through improved one-step hydrothermal method [23] by adding GO as an auxiliary reagent. Briefly, 100 mg of l-cysteine, 0.5 mL NH3·H2O (25%) and 4 mL GO dispersion (3.3 mg/mL) were dispersed in 6 mL deionized water. After ultrasonic oscillation for 10 min, the mixture was transferred into reaction kettle and heated at 180 °C for 3 h. When the reaction kettle was cooled down to room temperature, the solution was filtered to remove the solid and N, S-CQDs powder was obtained by freeze-drying the solution. 0.15 mg/mL yellow N, S-CQDs solution was prepared with deionized water and stored at 4 °C (Scheme 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experiment of fluorescent detections of different ions
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of the prepared N, S-CQDs was adjusted to 0.03 mg/mL in buffer solution in the following experiment. To detect the response of the N, S-CQDs to different pH, the suspensions were adjusted to different pH values by buffer and the fluorescence intensity were recorded by fluorescence spectrophotometer. Different cationic aqueous solutions (1 mM), i.e. Fe3+, Al3+, Ni2+, NH4 +, Cr3+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Cu2+, Hg2+ and K+ were prepared for fluorescence testing, respectively. Besides, different anion aqueous solutions (1 mM), i.e. F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, H2PO2 −, C2O4 −, CO3 2−, M2O4 2−, Sodium acetate (NaAC), HCO3 −, NO2 −, SO4 2−, Sodium citrate (NaCA) and NO3 − were also prepared for fluorescence testing. In addition, to detect the response time of the N, S-CQDs towards HMIs, the fluorescence spectra of the mixture of N, S-CQDs and HMIs were recorded at different times after adding in HMIs. The solutions of 100 μM citric acid, 100 μM K2HPO4, 100 μM KH2PO4, 40 μM H3PO4, 40 μM CH3COOH, 40 μM H3BO3, 200 μM NaOH, 100 μM NaCl were used to prepare the buffer solutions with different pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of removal efficiency of different ions by ICP-MS
ce:section.ce:para: The removal efficiency could be calculated by determination of different ions with ICP-MS. To obtain the removal efficiency of the N, S-CQDs to different ions, according to the national standard (GB8978-88), 0.03 mg/mL N, S-CQDs solution containing multiple HMIs were prepared. The mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 25 min. The concentration of HMIs in supernatant was detected by ICP-MS.

The PLQY measurement was accomplished by the standard relative method, in which the well-known quinine sulfate was used as the reference substance [24]. To determine the PLQY of N, S-CQDs, the integrated fluorescence intensity (excited at 330 nm) and absorbance (the absorbance at the excitation wavelength was tailored to be less than 0.1) of the N, S-CQDs solutions were compared with those of the standard quinine sulfate (dissolving it in 100 μM H2SO4 aqueous solution). To evaluate the PLQY value, the slope value was calculated [25]. The detailed PLQY (i.e. Φ
PL ) was obtained according to the followed equation:
Φ
P L
=
Φ
S T
(
I x
I
S T
)
(
η x
η
S T
)
2
× 100 %
where Φ
PL is the PLQY, I means the slope of the curves and η means the refractive index. The subscript “ST” refers to the standard substance, and “X” refers to the N, S-CQDs.

The morphology of the as-prepared N, S-CQDs was investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Jeol, Jeol JEM-2010). The crystal lattice of the N, S-CQDs was obtained by high magnification transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (Jeol, Jeol JEM-2010). The element mapping of the samples was carried out by energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) for the bright field scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) (Jeol, Jeol JEM-2010). The absorbance of the N, S-CQDs was measured by using UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV–Vis) (SHIMADZU, UV-1780). PL spectra were obtained by fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, F-4600) using xenon lamp as the excitation source (excitation wavelength: 330 nm). Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectrum was measured by Infrared spectrometer and infrared microscope combined system (TENSOR II + Hyperion2000, Bruker), with the purpose to determine the characteristic fingerprint signals of various functional groups of the N, S-CQDs. Binding energy of different elements, i.e. C1s, N1s, O1s, S2p, were conducted by commercial X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) equipment (ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Fisher). ICP-MS (PerkinElmer, NexION 300) was used to detect the residual content of different ions in the supernatant of N, S-CQDs reacted with specific ions and multi-ion solutions.

As shown in Fig. 1 , TEM analysis confirms the morphology of N, S-CQDs is close to spherical morphology and distributed evenly (Fig. 1a), showing relative narrow size distribution between 4 nm and 10 nm (Fig. 1b). Accordingly, the average size of N, S-CQDs is calculated to be 7.07 ± 1.47 nm. The HRTEM image is depicted in inset of Fig. 1a. As measured, the lattice spacing of such N, S-CQDs is 0.192 nm, corresponding to a diamond-like structure of the N, S-CQDs, which is quite similar with previously reported carbon dots [17,20,24]. Fig. 1c and d shows the morphology of the aggregates formed by mixing N, S-CQDs with Co2+ and Pb2+ respectively. It is obviously that addition of different metal ions causes the aggregation of the N, S-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 1c and d, different shapes of the aggregates were formed for different ions, e.g. big balls for Co2+ and dendritic aggregates for Pb2+, which probably be caused by the different coordination structures of Co2+ and Pb2+. The different morphology could also be found for Fe3+-N, S-CQDs, Cu2+-N, S-CQDs, Hg2+-N, S-CQDs (Figure S1).

The photophysical properties of N, S-CQDs are shown in Fig. 1e. The UV–vis adsorption spectrum of N, S-CQDs shows a weak absorption peaks at about 300 nm, which should be attributed to the n ∼ π* transition. Besides, the PL emission of N, S-CQDs was centered at ca. 400 nm when it was excited at 330 nm. In fluorescence excitation spectrum, only one excitation peak is observed for N, S-CQDs, indicating the structure of N, S-CQDs is a carbon quantum dots derived from l-cysteine rather than graphene oxide [26]. Taking the quinine sulfate solution in 100 μM H2SO4 (λex = 330 nm) as the standard substance, the PL quantum yield (PLQY) of N, S-CQDs was measured as 7.48%. The relatively low PLQY further indicates that the structure of N, S-CQDs is carbon quantum dot (typically PLQY <10%), while graphene quantum dots usually give higher PLQY [15]. The optical band gap is estimated to be ≈ 3.48 eV by the onset position of the absorption spectrum. In addition, the fluorescence properties of such N, S-CQDs at different pH were investigated. As shown in Fig. 1f, N, S-CQDs exhibit relatively stable fluorescence intensity in the range of pH 4–11, which is satisfactory for ion detection at a wide range of acidity. Besides, the fluorescence intensity of the N, S-CQDs are relatively photostable under xenon lamp (Figure S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of N, S-CQDs for detection of HMIs
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared N, S-CQDs were used to detect ions based on fluorescence spectroscopy, which showed wide scope of detecting capability on HMIs in water. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the N, S-CQDs showed fluorescence-off response to metal ions such as Co2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Pd2+, Cd2+ and Cr3+. Further, in order to investigate the selectivity of N, S-CQDs for heavy metal ions, the change of fluorescence intensity was measured with various anions and cations, including K+, Mg2+, Na+, Zn2+, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, H2PO2 −, C2O4 −, CO3 2−, M2O4 2−, AC−, HCO3 −, NO2 −, SO4 2−, CA− and NO3 −. It is demonstrated good selectivity for HMIs that fluorescence intensity showed negligible change toward above interferent ions as shown in Fig. 2a and b. The fluorescence intensity of N, S-CQDs could not be enhanced by any ion that mentioned above. Therefore, it could be concluded that the N, S-CQDs fluorescence probe is a HMIs-selective off-type fluorescence probe.

The fluorescence response of N, S-CQDs for Ni2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Pb2+and Co2+ ions with different concentrations were further investigated. With increasing the concentration of HMIs, the fluorescence intensity of the N, S-CQDs reduced gradually (Figure S2). Fig. 2c-h showed the linear dependence of ΔF/F0 on concentration of HMIs (Co2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Hg2+ and Cu2+). One in particular is the Co2+ ion, two linear response range was observed, i.e. in the range from 0 to 10 μM (R2 = 0.985) and from 10 to 70 μM (R2 = 0.998) (Fig. 2c), which could be attributed to the multiple coordination states of Co2+. With further increasing the concentration of Co2+ (e.g. larger than 70 μM), the degree of fluorescence attenuation decreased gradually, indicating that the fluorescent quenching is saturated at this condition. According to Fig. 2c-h, the limit of detection (LOD) (3σ/m, σ is the standard deviation and m is the slope of linear fitting) of Co2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Hg2+ and Ni2+ were calculated to be 0.85 μM, 3.20 μM, 4.52 μM, 2.02 μM, 3.27 μM and 2.39 μM, respectively. The LOD of this work are compared with the previously reported sensing materials and summarized in Table 1 . The result further demonstrates that the preferable performance of N, S-CQDs for HMIs detection. The standard deviation σ could be found in Table S1. Most of the σ values are smaller than 2%, indicating the precision of this fluorescence method is excellent.

To get the response time of the method, the decline of fluorescence intensity of N, S-CQDs after adding in HMIs was tested. The fluorescence intensity declined rapidly and basically stable after 5 min for Co2+ (Figure S4). For other HMIs, fluorescence quenching of N, S-CQDs occurs immediately after the addition of heavy metal ions. The fluorescence intensity was reduced to a minimum value and stabilized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for detecting metal ions by N, S-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the origin of the multi-ion detection capability, FT-IR spectra were taken to reveal the functional groups and the components of N, S-CQDs with and without HMIs. As shown in Fig. 3 , two strong peaks located at 3409 cm−1 and 3198 cm−1 were detected in the FT-IR spectrum of N, S-CQDs, which should be attributed to the stretching vibrations of –OH and –NH bonds [33]. As for the peaks at 3046 cm−1 and 1613 cm−1, they could be assigned to the stretching vibration of the unsaturated C–H bond and the stretching vibration of CC and CO [34]. respectively. The peak at 1394 cm−1 was observed and related to the stretching vibration of C–O or C–N confirming the doping of nitrogen [35]. In addition, the weak peak observed at 1134 cm−1 relates to the stretching vibration of CS [36] and the weak peak at 670 cm−1 corresponds to C–S bending vibration [37]. These results well confirm that nitrogen and sulfur elements are efficiently doped into the CQDs, i.e. the achievement of N, S-CQDs. The FT-IR spectra of the mixture of HMIs and N, S-CQDs (just Pb2+ is show here, others are shown in Figure S5) are showed in Fig. 3, comparing with N, S-CQDs, the peaks C–S bending vibration (about 670 nm) moves to longer wavelength direction in the mixture of Pb2+ and N, S-CQDs (Pb–N, S-CQDs), indicating Pb2+ may interact with S when detecting Pb2+.

To further study the detection mechanism for HMIs, XPS spectra of N, S-CQDs were measured to reveal the detailed chemical state of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and sulfur elements of N, S-CQDs and the mixture of HMIs and N, S-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 4 , for C1s (Fig. 4a and e), there are five main peaks exhibited at 284.5 eV, 285.2 eV, 286.1 eV, 287.8eV and 288.6 eV, which could be attributed to sp2 carbon (C–C, CC), sp3 carbon (C–O, C–N, C–S), OC–O and CO [36,38,39] respectively. For O1s (Fig. 4b), the peak divides into two peaks, i.e. 531.0 eV and 532.2 eV, which could be assigned to CO and C–O in chemical state [40]. Comparing with the XPS spectra of N, S-CQDs, the spectra of C1s and O1s remain the same after mixing with Pb2+ (Fig. 4e and f). The XPS results of N1s (Fig. 4c) revealed the presence of –NH (401.2 eV), N–(C)3 (400.5 eV), and C–N–C (399.3 eV) [41], which further confirmed that the CQDs are nitrogen-doped in composition [42]. Fig. 4d shows that two pairs of signals at 163.0 eV and 164.2 eV, 167.5 eV and 168.5 eV were found for S2p respectively, which might be assigned to the S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 of thiophene and C‒SOx [33,34,43,44]. A weak peak could be found at 161.0 eV which could be attributed to the S bonded with metal because of metallic contamination in Fig. 4d. Comparing with the XPS spectra of N, S-CQDs, the spectra of N1s and S2p found in Pb–N, S-CQDs have changed significantly. For N1s spectra, comparing to N, S-CQDs (Fig. 4c), the C–N–C peak at 399.3 eV of Pb–N, S-CQDs (Fig. 4g) decreased when bonded with HMIs [45]. As shown in Fig. 4d and h, the increased metal-S species at 161.0eV indicated that the detectability of N, S-CQDs could be attributed to the electro interaction between S and HMIs.

In traditional, for carbon dots and graphene dots, the mechanism for selectively detect certain ions is believed that the negatively charged dots along with O–H, –COOH and –CO could bind with certain ions, resulting in the aggregation of carbon dots and [46]. However, as for the detection of multiple HMIs of N, S-CQDs, we propose the mechanism that the aggregation caused by coordination interaction between metals and sulfur. According to the FT-IR and XPS spectra, it is speculated that when HMIs are present in the solution, S in C–S bond of the N, S-CQDs would coordinated with HMIs, which quenched the excitons for radiative emission, i.e. monotonous decreasing the fluorescence of the N, S-CQDs. To prove the conjecture, the bright field STEM of the sample of Pb–N, S-CQDs were conducted. As shown in STEM images (Fig. 5 ), the elemental mapping of S, O, N, and Pb shows that Pb is homogeneously distributed throughout the aggregated N, S-CQDs. The distribution of Pb is in consisted with the distribution of S element. UV–vis adsorption spectra further prove that the aggregation is induced by coordination between S and HMIs. As shown in Figure S6, the UV–vis absorption intensity at 350–500 nm increases gradually, which relates to the adsorption of charge transfer transition from ligands to d orbital of metals [47], with the increase of HMIs. Here, the N, S-CQDs are electron donor and HMIs with d-orbital are electron acceptor. This phenomenon confirms the formation of coordination compounds who is non-fluorescent. And the coordination mechanism further explained the reason for selectively detecting HMIs rather than those ions who do not have d orbital, e.g. K+, Na+ etc.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the N, S-CQDs for detecting ions in real water sample
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the suitability and practical application in detection of environmental water quality. In practice, the N, S-CQDs were applied to determine total content of HMIs in water. Spike-and-recovery experience was adopted to determine content of HMIs in water [48]. The tap water was from our laboratory (Guangzhou, Guangdong, China), the river water was from Zhujiang river (Guangzhou, Guangdong, China), all the water samples used in the experiment was centrifuged and filtered. The active N of the experiment of the determination of different ions in real water samples was 3. The results are showed in table S1. It is shown in Figure S8 that the fluorescence of N, S-CQDs was obviously quenched by the multiple-ions-involved water in which the concentration of individuals meets the Chinese national standard (GB8978-88). This proved that the as-synthesized N, S-CQDs could be used to evaluated the water quality in real water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N, S-CQDs in removing heavy metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the removal efficiency and practical application the HMIs in purification of environmental water quality. It is shown in Table S2 that the concentration of Co2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Pb2+ and Cu2+ decreases significantly after adding N, S-CQDs. We also found that the removal efficiency of Pd2+ is dependent on concentration of Pd2+. At higher concentration of Pb2+, e.g., 20 μΜ, Pd2+ could be substantially removed and the concentration of Pb2+ of the supernatant meets the national standard (GB8978-88). The removal efficiency of Pb2+ is up to ∼93.6%. These results prove that the as-synthesized N, S-CQDs is an excellent HMIs removal agent to optimize the water quality in real water samples.

In summary, an excellent N, S-CQDs fluorescence probe for detection and capture different HMIs were synthesized successfully by graphene oxide-assisted synthesis method. For detecting HMIs, the N, S-CQDs exhibited a good linear response in a wide range for most heavy metal ions in water with extremely low LOD, including Co2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, and Pb2+ etc. It is proved that the aggregation caused by coordination interaction between HMIs and N, S-CQDs is responsible for the fluorescence quenching response of N, S-CQDs. Moreover, for the detections of HMIs, the N, S-CQDs had been successfully applied to actual water sources with a satisfactory sensitivity. Moreover, the N, S-CQDs could be used to remove HMIs from the real water samples. Therefore, the N, S-CQDs are expected to be a new material, which can not only be used to detect the HMIs, but also provide a platform for metal capture.

Yujie Shen: Writing – original draft; Formal analysis; Methodology. Mingcong Rong: Methodology. Xiaodan Qu: Validation. Bolin Zhao: Validation. Jinhui Zou: Formal analysis. Zhenbang Liu: Software. Yu Bao: Software. Ying He: Funding acquisition. Siyi Li: Visualization. Xiaojin Wang: Visualization. Meiyun Chen: Visualization. Kaiying Chen: Visualization. Yuwei Zhang: Conceptualization; Methodology; Supervision; Project administration; Writing – review & editing. Li Niu: Supervision; Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Research & Development Projects in Key Areas of Guangdong Province, China (no. 2019B010933001), National Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 21974033, no. 21904026), Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (no. 2021B1515020048) and Guangzhou Science and Technology Plan Project (no. 202102010390).

ce:section.ce:para:
